forecaster_note: A big, powerful dead-south groundswell is the story today — 10ft @ 10s from 181°, firing ~1760kJ (peaking near 2090). That dead-S angle is exactly the direction that wraps into Dee Why poorly, so the sheltered Dee Why corner holds clean, manageable 2–4ft under a light SW offshore, while the S-exposed beaches get overrun: Long Reef 4–6ft but onshore and messy, Curl Curl 4–5ft cross-shore. It's a classic 'small-but-clean vs big-but-blown' morning — the corner beats the size. The swell fades through Thursday and Saturday goes tiny and showery, before Sunday rebuilds with a friendlier ENE (75°) pulse that finally suits the Point. — Surf Guru, read from Surfline model data (no written report scraped this run).

wind.summary: Light-to-moderate SSW ~22 km/h, gusting 31 — offshore and clean at the E-facing corners (Dee Why, Manly), cross-to-onshore on the S-exposed beaches. Best early before it firms up.

shark.summary: SharkSmart detection feed not available this refresh — the live SMART-drumline/detection service returned an error (503). Water temp still read fine (19°C). Tap through to check live detections before you paddle out.

days.label: Today
days.note: Powerful dead-south swell — 10ft @ 10s from 181°, ~1760kJ. The dead-S angle wraps into Dee Why poorly, so paradoxically the sheltered Dee Why corner is your best clean option (2–4ft, light SW offshore) while the open beaches are big and blown. Best window is mid-morning off the 8:56am high into the dropping tide, before the SSW firms up. 🦈 Note: the SharkSmart detection feed was down this run (503) — check the live map before you go in.

swells.label: Dead-S groundswell — wraps into the corner, big offshore washes through

dee-why.window: Your pick today. The dead-S angle keeps the corner a clean, workable 2–4ft while the open beaches max out. Light SW offshore grooms it — surf the mid-morning window off the high before the wind firms.

tip.label: Frontside rights
tip.text: 1760kJ of dead-S energy wraps into Dee Why Point — frontside walls for your natural stance. It's powerful for the size, so sit a touch deeper and use the offshore to hold your line off the top. Corner is cleaner than the exposed banks all morning.

swells.label: Dead-S groundswell — reef takes it head-on, well overhead sets

long-reef.window: Biggest of the four — the S-facing reef copes the full dead-S swell at 4–6ft, but the SSW blows straight onshore here, so it's bumpy and heavy. For experienced paddlers chasing size only.

tip.label: Size & caution
tip.text: 42/100 consistency at 4–6ft with onshore texture — powerful but disorganised. Backside on the lefts here; step up in volume and pick your sets rather than fighting the chop.

swells.label: Dead-S groundswell — S-magnet beach loads up, cross-shore texture

curl-curl.window: Solid 4–5ft overhead peaks but the SSW sits cross-shore, so faces are sectiony rather than clean. Punchy if you catch a set between the bumps.

tip.label: Punchy but cross
tip.text: Same 1760kJ punch as the others — plenty of power, but 45/100 and cross-shore means you're hunting the odd clean one. Step-up board, commit early on the sets.

swells.label: Dead-S groundswell — north-end shelter trims it right down

manly.window: The most protected corner — the north-end shelter knocks the dead-S down to a clean, playful 2–3ft with the SSW offshore. The mellow, forgiving alternative to Dee Why.

tip.label: Clean & smaller
tip.text: Offshore and only chest-to-head — the easy pick if you want quantity over power. Everyday board; work the North Steyne banks on the dropping tide.

days.label: Tomorrow
days.note: The south swell eases and shortens — 9ft @ 8s from 177°, energy down to ~890kJ. Dee Why stays a modest 2–4ft; the exposed beaches drop to 4–5ft but a stronger SSW (14kt, gusting 21) sits cross-to-onshore, so it's messier than today. Dawn before the wind is the only clean window. Manly the tidy small option.

dee-why.window: Similar size to today but the freshening SSW turns cross-shore, so it loses the clean edge. Get in at dawn or wait for the weekend.

long-reef.window: Still overhead but onshore and disorganised — heavy paddle for little reward. One to skip unless it's the only slot you've got.

curl-curl.window: Overhead peaks but cross-shore bump — snag it at first light before the SSW gets into it, otherwise it's a wash.

manly.window: Trimmed to waist-to-shoulder by the north-end shelter, but the SSW is offshore here — the cleanest of a middling Thursday.

days.label: Weekend
days.note: Two very different days. SATURDAY goes tiny and showery — the old swell fades to ~70kJ, 1–3ft and poor, with light onshore. SUNDAY is the one: a fresh ENE pulse (7.5ft @ 10s from 75°) rebuilds energy to ~620kJ. That 75° angle sits right in your favoured NE–ENE window and wraps into Dee Why far better than this week's dead-S — winds only light onshore, so glassy early. Values below reflect Sunday; save your session for it.

dee-why.window: Sunday's pick. The ENE 75° angle finally feeds the Point the way dead-S never does — go early on the light-onshore glass for fun frontside rights. Saturday's near flat, so don't bother till Sunday.

long-reef.window: Bigger again on Sunday off the reef, but the light onshore keeps it textured — solid rather than clean.

curl-curl.window: Fun head-high peaks Sunday if you want a bit more wall than Dee Why — light onshore, so early is best.

manly.window: Steady chest-to-head Sunday and the most sheltered from the light onshore — the safe, tidy option if Dee Why's crowded.
